serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese chicken fried rice noodles is approximately 300 g, typically served as a single-plate meal.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice Noodles with Chicken (estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Rice noodles cooked, Chicken stir-fried, and FAO/INFOODS SEA composite)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Burmese kyar zan kyaw exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values estimated by weighted composite: approximately 55% cooked rice vermicelli (USDA FDC #169726), 25% stir-fried chicken breast (USDA FDC #171477), 10% egg (USDA FDC #173424), 10% vegetables and condiments (bean sprouts, spring onion, soy sauce, fish sauce, oil). Macros cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA stir-fried noodle analogues.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, cooked — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Burmese fried rice noodle dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese kyar zan kyaw. GI of 53 derived from University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low category). Stir-frying with protein (chicken, egg) and fat (oil) further attenuates postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 20.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 300g serving) / 100 = approximately 33 g. gi_category set to low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of rice vermicelli (GI ~53) is partially offset by a high glycemic load per serving (~33 g) due to large portion size; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced portion of approximately 150–200 g and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from stir-frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) and moderate fat from stir-frying oil present a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request lower-oil preparation and limit portion to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kyar Zan Kyaw (ကြာဇံကြော်) with chicken is a popular Burmese stir-fried noodle dish made from thin rice vermicelli noodles (kyar zan) wok-tossed at high heat with sliced or shredded chicken, egg, bean sprouts, spring onion, garlic, soy sauce, and fish sauce, finished with a drizzle of sesame or cooking oil. The dish has a light, slightly smoky wok-hei character, with tender noodles, savoury umami depth from the sauces, and a mild sweetness from the bean sprouts. It is a staple street food and restaurant dish across Myanmar and is commonly served as a single-plate lunch or dinner. In Singapore, it is offered at Myanmar restaurants such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 486 kcal, 25.5 g protein, 15.6 g fat, and 62.4 g carbohydrate. The rice vermicelli base has a low glycemic index (~53), making it a relatively moderate-GI option compared to wheat noodles, though the large serving size results in a high glycemic load (~33 g). Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and fish sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and typically halal when prepared with halal chicken.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Burmese kyar zan kyaw (chicken) exists in any primary approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FDC SR Legacy entries for cooked rice vermicelli, stir-fried chicken breast, egg, and condiments. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli as the closest analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FDC SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish uses vegetable cooking oil with no hydrogenated fat components. GI set to 53 based on University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli analogue; gi_category is low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). GL per serving = (53 × 20.8 × 300) / 10000 = 33.0 g. The food_group 'Grains and staples' is preserved from the input record as the primary component is rice vermicelli. Cuisine tags 'Lor Mee' and 'Noodle' from the field survey source are noted but not adopted as canonical descriptors; kyar zan kyaw is a distinct Burmese dish and should not be conflated with Singaporean lor mee.
